content.text: The chat completions endpoint is the foundational HTTP interface for conversational language model inference. It accepts an ordered list of role-tagged messages, runs a model forward pass over the entire context, and returns generated assistant text -- or streams it token-by-token as Server-Sent Events. Nearly every agent orchestration framework, from LangChain to custom loops, ultimately resolves to this API shape.
The chat completions API is a stateless REST endpoint (canonically

) that maps a structured request -- a list of messages, a model identifier, and generation parameters -- to a response containing the model's reply, token usage statistics, and a finish reason. Despite the name, 'chat' is a misnomer: the API handles single-turn inference, multi-turn conversation, tool-augmented reasoning, and structured JSON output equally well.

OpenAI introduced this shape in 2023 and it has since become the de-facto interoperability standard for LLM providers. Anthropic's Messages API, Mistral, Groq, Together AI, Ollama, and local runtimes like vLLM all expose a compatible or identical contract. Learning this one interface unlocks access to dozens of models and providers without re-learning a new SDK for each.
The request body is JSON with four mandatory fields:
(an ordered array of role/content pairs), and usually
(output length cap). Optional fields control sampling (
), tool schemas, streaming, and provider-specific extensions like cache hints or reasoning budget.

Agent architectures are built on repeated chat completion calls. The canonical agent loop is: system prompt + message history + latest user input -> model proposes an action (or final text) -> runtime executes the action -> result is appended to history -> call again. Every iteration is one chat completion request. Understanding the request and response structure is therefore a prerequisite for implementing retries, streaming UX, tool dispatch, cost accounting, and context window management.

Beyond agents, the API is the entry point for RAG pipelines that inject retrieved documents as context, few-shot prompting where example dialogues precede the user query, chain-of-thought workflows that inspect intermediate reasoning, and structured extraction tasks that constrain output to JSON schemas. Mastering this API unlocks the full capability surface of modern LLMs.
Concretely, failing to understand the API leads to real production bugs: silently truncated responses when
; broken tool loops when the assistant message containing the tool call is dropped from history; runaway spend from missing
caps; and degraded latency from unnecessarily large prompts resent on every turn.
-- the same message-array shape works across providers; swapping models often requires changing only the
enables token-by-token delivery via SSE, giving users perceptible responsiveness even for long outputs.
-- tool schemas and tool results are first-class citizens of the same request/response, eliminating the need for separate orchestration protocols.
with a JSON schema constrains generation to a valid object using constrained decoding on supported models.
for per-call cost tracking and context window bookkeeping.
-- SDKs in Python, TypeScript, Go, Java, and Rust; middleware like LiteLLM that normalises provider differences; local runtimes for offline or private deployments.
Understanding the limitations is as important as knowing the capabilities, because each one has a direct engineering consequence.

-- the API holds no server-side session. History is entirely the client's responsibility: every turn must resend all prior messages. Input tokens grow with conversation length, increasing both latency and cost linearly.

-- the time to first token (TTFT) grows roughly linearly with prompt token count because the model must attend over every prior token before generating the first new one. Long histories hurt interactive latency.
Context window is a hard ceiling
-- models have a fixed context limit (e.g. 128K tokens for GPT-4o). Exceeding it returns a 400 error; the client must implement truncation or summarisation.
-- reasoning tokens (OpenAI o-series), cache_control hints (Anthropic), seed for reproducibility, and logprobs differ across providers. Portable code must abstract or conditionally apply these.
-- the API is pure inference. External memory stores, vector databases, and retrieval layers must be plumbed in by the application.
-- providers throttle requests per-minute and tokens-per-minute. Agent loops with high call frequency must implement backoff and request queuing.
When a client sends a chat completion request the provider performs these steps:
: the messages array is serialised with a model-specific chat template and converted to a flat sequence of integer token IDs. The template injects role markers (e.g.
: the full token sequence, including system prompt and entire history, is fed through all transformer layers to compute key-value (KV) cache entries for every position. Prefill cost is
in prompt length and dominates TTFT for long contexts.
: the model samples one token at a time, appending each to the sequence and re-running the final layer. Cost per step is approximately
because the KV cache reuses prior computations.
: after each token the runtime checks for an EOS token, a stop sequence,
exhaustion, or a tool-call pattern. The run halts and sets
: generated tokens are decoded back to text and wrapped in the response JSON (or SSE stream if
The messages array is an ordered list of objects. Each has a
-- a privileged preamble injected before any user message. Sets persona, constraints, and task context. Never visible to the end user.
-- input from the human turn. May contain text, images (multimodal models), or base64-encoded documents.
-- a prior model reply. Re-send previous assistant messages to give the model access to its own prior output. Also used to inject a partial prefix to guide generation.
-- the result of a function called by the model. Must be appended after the assistant message that requested the call. The
A successful non-streaming response has this shape (simplified):
When the model decides to call a tool,
contains an array of objects with
(a JSON string). The client must parse arguments, execute the function, and append a tool-role message before calling the API again.

The diagram below shows the three main actors in a tool-augmented agent loop: the client that maintains message history, the provider API that runs inference, and the tool executor that bridges model decisions to real-world effects. Streaming (SSE) is shown as an optional path from the provider directly back to the client.

attrs.alt: Chat Completions API architecture: client, provider API, and tool executor showing the request/response/tool-call loop

content.text: The key insight from the diagram is that the provider API is truly stateless: every arrow into it carries the full conversation history. The client is responsible for appending each new message (user input, assistant reply, tool result) before the next call. This design lets providers scale horizontally -- any server can handle any request -- at the cost of growing payload sizes over long conversations.
The chat completions API is the right choice when conversation structure, tool use, or multi-turn context are part of the problem:
Building a multi-turn conversational agent where the model must reference prior turns to answer correctly.
Implementing tool-augmented reasoning (ReAct, function-calling loops) where the model selects and invokes external functions.
RAG pipelines that inject retrieved passages as user-role context blocks before the actual query.
constrains output to a validated JSON schema.
Few-shot prompting where example input/output pairs are encoded as alternating user/assistant messages.
The task is single-shot classification or embedding -- use the embeddings endpoint or a dedicated classifier to avoid the overhead of full generative inference.
You need to process thousands of independent prompts offline -- the Batch API offers a 50% cost reduction.
The output is a fixed-format extraction with no multi-turn dependency -- a single system+user call with a JSON schema is sufficient; no history management is needed.
Step 1 -- Install the SDK and set credentials

pip install openai          # OpenAI / compatible providers
pip install anthropic       # Anthropic Claude
export OPENAI_API_KEY='sk-...'
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY='sk-ant-...'

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from environment

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are a concise technical assistant.'},
        {'role': 'user',   'content': 'What does the finish_reason field mean?'},
    ],
    temperature=0.2,
    max_tokens=256,
)

# Always inspect finish_reason before using the content
choice = response.choices[0]
if choice.finish_reason == 'stop':
    print(choice.message.content)
elif choice.finish_reason == 'length':
    raise RuntimeError('Response truncated -- increase max_tokens or truncate prompt')

print('Tokens used:', response.usage.total_tokens)

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

def run_conversation(system: str) -> None:
    messages = [{'role': 'system', 'content': system}]

    while True:
        user_input = input('You: ').strip()
        if not user_input:
            break

        messages.append({'role': 'user', 'content': user_input})

        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model='gpt-4o-mini',
            messages=messages,
            max_tokens=512,
        )

        assistant_message = response.choices[0].message
        # Append the full message object so the model sees its own
        # prior replies on the next turn.
        messages.append({'role': 'assistant', 'content': assistant_message.content})

        print(f'Assistant: {assistant_message.content}')

run_conversation('You are a knowledgeable Python tutor.')

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()

stream = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Explain tokenisation in three paragraphs.'}],
    stream=True,
    max_tokens=512,
)

collected = []
for chunk in stream:
    delta = chunk.choices[0].delta
    if delta.content:
        print(delta.content, end='', flush=True)
        collected.append(delta.content)

full_response = ''.join(collected)
print()  # newline after stream
Force the model to return a validated JSON object by setting
to a JSON schema. This is useful for parsing unstructured text into typed records without post-processing heuristics.

from openai import OpenAI
import json

client = OpenAI()

TEXT = (
    'John Smith joined Acme Corp in March 2021 as a Senior Engineer. '
    'He works remotely from Vancouver and his employee ID is EMP-4492.'
)

SCHEMA = {
    'type': 'json_schema',
    'json_schema': {
        'name': 'employee_record',
        'schema': {
            'type': 'object',
            'properties': {
                'name':        {'type': 'string'},
                'company':     {'type': 'string'},
                'start_date':  {'type': 'string'},
                'role':        {'type': 'string'},
                'location':    {'type': 'string'},
                'employee_id': {'type': 'string'},
            },
            'required': ['name', 'company', 'start_date', 'role'],
            'additionalProperties': False,
        },
        'strict': True,
    },
}

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'Extract employee information as JSON.'},
        {'role': 'user',   'content': TEXT},
    ],
    response_format=SCHEMA,
    max_tokens=256,
    temperature=0,
)

record = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
print(record)
# {'name': 'John Smith', 'company': 'Acme Corp', 'start_date': 'March 2021',
#  'role': 'Senior Engineer', 'location': 'Vancouver', 'employee_id': 'EMP-4492'}

The Anthropic API differs in that the system prompt is a top-level parameter rather than a message role, and the response shape uses a content block list instead of a choices array. The same conceptual multi-turn pattern applies.

from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY

def run_claude_conversation(system: str, history: list, user_input: str) -> str:
    history.append({'role': 'user', 'content': user_input})

    response = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-sonnet-4-5',
        system=system,
        messages=history,
        max_tokens=512,
    )

    assistant_text = response.content[0].text
    history.append({'role': 'assistant', 'content': assistant_text})
    return assistant_text

history = []
system  = 'You are a helpful code reviewer. Be concise.'

turn1 = run_claude_conversation(system, history, 'Review this: x = 1 + "2"')
print('Turn 1:', turn1)

turn2 = run_claude_conversation(system, history, 'How would you fix it?')
print('Turn 2:', turn2)

print('Total history length:', len(history), 'messages')
A customer support agent deployed at a SaaS company handles 200-500 concurrent conversations. Each conversation follows this pattern:
A system prompt (~800 tokens) describes product policies, escalation rules, and a persona. It is prepended to every request.
On each user message, the runtime fetches conversation history from Redis (capped at the last 20 turns to stay under token limits) and calls the completions API with a tool for ticket creation.

If the model calls the ticket tool, the runtime creates the ticket in the external system, appends the tool result, and makes a second completion call to generate the user-facing confirmation message.
The final assistant message is streamed to the chat widget via SSE and written back to Redis with a 1-hour TTL.

In production this pattern typically uses a worker pool (asyncio with httpx or Celery) to handle concurrent conversations, a token counter to enforce the history cap, and exponential backoff on 429 responses. Average latency per turn is 800 ms - 2 s at GPT-4o-mini with streaming enabled, with TTFT around 200 ms.
Dropping the assistant message that contains tool calls
array that links each tool call to its ID. Omitting this message before appending the tool result causes a validation error because the tool result references an ID with no corresponding request.

# WRONG -- drops the assistant message that contains the tool_calls array
messages.append({'role': 'tool', 'tool_call_id': tc.id, 'content': result})

# RIGHT -- append assistant first, then the tool result
messages.append(choice.message)          # role=assistant + tool_calls
messages.append({
    'role':         'tool',
    'tool_call_id':  tc.id,
    'content':       result,
})
Treating the response as complete when
produces silently truncated output. For JSON structured outputs this causes parse errors downstream.

# WRONG -- ignores possible truncation
content = response.choices[0].message.content

# RIGHT -- guard on finish_reason before using content
choice = response.choices[0]
if choice.finish_reason not in ('stop', 'tool_calls'):
    raise ValueError(f'Unexpected finish_reason: {choice.finish_reason}')
content = choice.message.content
Using high temperature for tool-calling agents
High temperature introduces stochastic variation in argument generation, making JSON arguments malformed or semantically wrong (e.g. wrong enum values). Use
for tool-calling and structured output tasks.
No timeout on the HTTP call
Without a timeout, a slow model or network partition blocks an entire worker thread indefinitely. Always set a read timeout appropriate for expected response length.

from openai import OpenAI

# Set a 60-second read timeout so slow responses do not block workers
client = OpenAI(timeout=60.0)
Appending the SDK message object directly
The SDK message object returned in
is a Pydantic model. Appending it to a list that is later JSON-serialised may fail silently or produce unexpected output. Convert to a plain dict first.

# Safer: convert Pydantic message to a plain dict before appending to list
messages.append(choice.message.model_dump(exclude_unset=True))
Retry on 429 and 5xx with exponential backoff
. A jittered backoff (base 1 s, max 60 s, 5 retries) handles transient provider outages. The OpenAI SDK retries automatically; set
explicitly rather than relying on the default.
Log usage fields on every call
, model, and timestamp in a structured log. Aggregate in a cost dashboard to detect runaway loops before they become expensive.

. Implement a sliding window or summarisation strategy once messages approach 50-60% of the model's context limit. Sliding windows are simpler; summarisation preserves more semantic content at lower token cost.
. Use Pydantic or JSON Schema validation against the same schema sent to the model. Never pass raw model output to a shell or database query.
Use idempotency keys for side-effecting tools
. Derive the key from the tool call ID and conversation ID so that retried calls do not double-send emails, create duplicate tickets, etc.
which can silently change behaviour when the provider updates the alias.
Test with temperature=0 and a fixed seed
during development to get reproducible outputs and catch regressions before random sampling makes failures intermittent.

End-to-end latency has two distinct phases: time to first token (TTFT) and time per output token (TPOT). TTFT is dominated by prefill and grows with prompt length. TPOT is roughly constant per model and hardware tier. For interactive applications, optimising TTFT matters most; for batch pipelines, total throughput (tokens/second) matters more.
Typical ranges for hosted models (subject to provider load and tier):
Most providers offer KV-cache reuse for prompt prefixes that repeat across calls. OpenAI's automatic prefix caching and Anthropic's
hints both reduce prefill cost when the system prompt is large and stable. Cache hits save 50-80% of prompt token cost and reduce TTFT proportionally to the cached fraction.
to run independent completions in parallel within one process.
For batch workloads without interactive latency requirements, use the provider's Batch API to process thousands of prompts at half the cost.
Horizontal scaling of the client tier is straightforward because each call is stateless. The bottleneck is usually the provider's tokens-per-minute quota, not client compute.
Rate-limit per tenant in multi-tenant systems to prevent one user's agent loop from consuming the whole organisation's quota.
(Python/TypeScript) -- official OpenAI SDK; also works with any OpenAI-compatible endpoint via
(Python/TypeScript) -- Anthropic Messages API with streaming helpers and type-safe message builders.
-- a proxy layer that translates between the OpenAI chat-completions shape and 100+ provider APIs including Cohere, Bedrock, Gemini, and local Ollama servers. Swap providers by changing one string.

# Run a local model with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint
# ollama serve &
# ollama pull llama3.2:3b

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(
    base_url='http://localhost:11434/v1',
    api_key='ollama',   # required field, ignored by Ollama
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='llama3.2:3b',
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Explain gradient descent briefly.'}],
    max_tokens=200,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

Production deployments benefit from request-level tracing. Libraries like LangSmith (LangChain), Weave (Weights & Biases), and Helicone (HTTP proxy) capture the full message array, latency, token usage, and tool calls without requiring changes to application code beyond a one-line SDK initialisation. These tools surface cost per conversation, latency percentiles, and tool-call error rates in dashboards.
The chat completions interface is not the only way to call a language model. The right choice depends on the task structure, latency budget, and cost constraints.

API keys grant direct access to billed quota. Never hardcode them in source code. Load from environment variables at runtime, or use a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager). Rotate keys after any potential exposure. Use per-team or per-service keys to scope blast radius and enable independent revocation.
Malicious user content can attempt to override system instructions, exfiltrate context from the system prompt, or trigger unintended tool calls. Mitigations:
Separate user-supplied content from privileged instructions using structural markers the system prompt instructs the model to treat differently.
Validate tool arguments against the declared schema before execution -- never pass raw model output to a shell or SQL interpreter.
For high-stakes tools (file deletion, financial operations), require human-in-the-loop confirmation before execution.
Providers apply content filters that can trigger
. Handle this gracefully -- do not surface the raw error to end users. For regulated industries, review provider data processing agreements before sending PII or PHI through the API.
Set read timeouts (60-120 s for long outputs) and handle
Use circuit breakers around the API call in high-volume services: open the circuit after N consecutive 5xx responses to shed load to a fallback model or cached response.
Log the full request (redacting PII) and response for post-hoc debugging; LLM failures are hard to reproduce without the original prompt.

Think of the chat completions API as a pure function: f(messages, params) -> (reply, finish_reason, usage). It has no side effects, no memory, and no identity between calls. The agent runtime is the stateful layer that wraps this pure function: it maintains the message list, decides when to stop, routes tool calls to real implementations, and enforces policies like max turns and context window limits.

A useful analogy: the API is like a knowledgeable consultant who reads the complete meeting transcript every time they are called into the room. They have perfect recall of everything written in the transcript, but zero memory of previous meetings unless those notes are included. The person running the meeting (the agent runtime) is responsible for keeping the transcript accurate and complete.

Every chat completion is stateless. The conversation exists only in the messages array. If a message is dropped, the model has no way to recover it -- it never existed as far as the model is concerned.
The chat completions API is a stateless function: it takes a full message array on every call and returns a reply; there is no server-side session state.
The messages array carries all context -- system instructions, user turns, prior assistant replies, and tool results must all be explicitly included on every request.
means the agent loop must dispatch a function before continuing.
Tool calling is a first-class feature: tool schemas go in the request, and tool results go back as role-
messages before the next completion call.
) dramatically improves perceived responsiveness by delivering tokens as they are generated via Server-Sent Events.
Context window growth is the main scalability challenge: implement a history cap or summarisation strategy before prompt tokens exceed 50-60% of the model limit.
Pin model version IDs in production, log usage fields on every call, and always validate tool arguments before execution.

The OpenAI chat completions shape has become the de-facto standard: LiteLLM, Ollama, vLLM, and most hosted providers expose a compatible API, making provider-switching a one-line change in most codebases.
